title: Securing the Hemispheric Infrastructure Battleground
content:
In the modern era, global infrastructure has emerged as the key battleground for geopolitical influence. Ports, logistics corridors, energy systems, telecommunications networks, and subsea cables are no longer merely neutral commercial assets; they are powerful instruments of statecraft. The United States cannot speak credibly about nearshoring or supply chain resilience while allowing critical infrastructure in the Western Hemisphere to be shaped by opaque financing and political dependency on strategic competitors. These vulnerabilities undermine reliable access to lithium, copper, rare earths, and other strategic inputs essential for advanced manufacturing and clean energy transitions. Therefore, securing the hemispheric infrastructure is crucial for maintaining both economic stability and national security.

Furthermore, recent foreign policy debates highlight the reluctance of major powers to assume sweeping control over foreign lands. Past efforts to do so have often failed to instill stability, instead sapping billions of dollars and untold goodwill. The prospect of prolonged foreign entanglement serves as a strong deterrent against unwise adventurism. As a result, Washington must strike a delicate balance between protecting its strategic interests and avoiding counterproductive overseas interventions. By focusing on building resilient domestic and regional supply chains, nations can better withstand external shocks and deter adversaries without the need for direct military confrontation.
